meta.title: The First 2,000 Days — India milestone timeline
meta.notes: Population-level ranges, not deadlines. isMilestoneCheck rows align to CDC checkpoint ages.

entries.description: Your newborn arrives with a set of automatic reflexes — rooting, sucking, grasping, and the startle (Moro) reflex. Vision is blurry and limited to about 8-12 inches, roughly the distance to your face during a feed.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Hold baby close during feeds — that 8-12 inch range is exactly where their focus works best.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby is not feeding at all in the first hours, or seems limp and unresponsive.
entries.notificationText: Day 1: hello world! Reflexes are online, vision is set to 'face distance.'

entries.description: Babies typically lose up to 7-10% of birth weight in the first days as they transition from colostrum to fuller milk supply. Meconium (the dark, tarry first stool) is being passed.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Frequent short feeds (8-12 times/24h) are normal and help bring in milk supply.
entries.doctorFlag: No wet or dirty diapers at all by day 2.
entries.notificationText: Day 2: first poops, first weight dip — both totally on schedule.

entries.description: Around now, milk often transitions from colostrum to a higher volume supply. Babies may seem more actively hungry, and audible swallowing during feeds becomes more common.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Watch for swallowing sounds, not just sucking, as a sign milk transfer is working.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby seems constantly frantic at the breast/bottle and never satisfied after 30+ minutes.
entries.notificationText: Day 3: milk's arriving — expect a hungrier, busier baby today.

entries.description: Feeding starts settling into a rough pattern of every 2-3 hours. Baby may briefly track a face or bright object before losing focus — early visual tracking in short bursts.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Talk and make eye contact during feeds; short tracking moments are a great time to connect.
entries.doctorFlag: Fewer than 6 wet diapers per day by the end of week one.
entries.notificationText: Day 4: baby's eyes are starting to follow you, just for a beat.

entries.description: The umbilical cord stump is drying out, and skin peeling is common and harmless as newborn skin adjusts to air. None of this reflects diet or health issues.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Keep the cord stump dry and let it fall off on its own — no need to speed it up.
entries.doctorFlag: Redness, swelling, or odor around the cord stump.
entries.notificationText: Day 5: peeling skin, drying cord — normal newborn housekeeping.

entries.description: You may catch a fleeting smile — but this is a reflexive, not social, smile at this age, often appearing during sleep or gas. The real social smile is still weeks away.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Enjoy it anyway — it's practice for the muscles that will soon do it on purpose.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby never opens their eyes or startles to loud sound at all.
entries.notificationText: Day 6: that flicker of a smile? Still reflex, not "hi Mom" yet.

entries.description: Sleep dominates the day — 16 to 18 hours total, in short stretches. Weight tends to bottom out around now before starting to climb back toward birth weight.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Sleep when baby sleeps this week; the fragmented pattern is temporary.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby hasn't regained any lost weight by the one-week pediatric visit.
entries.notificationText: Day 7: one week in. Sleep is chaotic; that's the design, not a problem.

entries.description: Newborn vision responds best to strong black-and-white contrast and simple patterns rather than pastel colors, which are hard to distinguish yet.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Try a high-contrast board book or card at feeding distance to hold attention.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby's eyes appear to cross constantly or one eye seems to wander persistently.
entries.notificationText: Day 8: baby's world is black, white, and blurry-beautiful.

entries.description: Twitches, jerky movements, and facial grimaces during sleep are part of "active sleep," a lighter REM-like stage newborns spend a lot of time in.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Resist the urge to intervene at every twitch — most of it is normal sleep architecture.
entries.doctorFlag: Repetitive jerking that doesn't stop when you touch or reposition baby.
entries.notificationText: Day 9: those sleep-twitches are baby's brain doing important wiring work.

entries.description: Most babies regain their birth weight by day 10-14. This is one of the most-watched early milestones at pediatric visits, alongside feeding and diaper counts.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: If you're formula or combo feeding, keep a rough log of diapers this week — it's the easiest read on intake.
entries.doctorFlag: No signs of weight regain trending by this point — worth a call to your pediatrician.
entries.notificationText: Day 10: the weight-regain race is nearly won.

entries.description: Baby now reacts more distinctly to loud or sudden sounds — not just the whole-body startle, but brief pauses or head turns toward noise.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Notice which sounds seem to soothe vs. startle — you're learning baby's sound preferences.
entries.doctorFlag: No reaction whatsoever to loud, close sounds.
entries.notificationText: Day 11: baby's ears are tuning in to the world's soundtrack.

entries.description: Brief, genuine eye contact during alert feeding moments is starting to appear — an early thread of connection before smiling develops.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Feed in a dim, quiet spot sometimes to make eye contact easier for baby to sustain.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby actively avoids gaze and seems distressed by any face-to-face contact.
entries.notificationText: Day 12: a few real seconds of eye contact today — soak it in.

entries.description: The palmar grasp reflex is strong now — baby's fingers curl tightly around anything pressed into their palm, including your finger.
entries.visionMilestone: Sees only in blurry high-contrast shapes at 8-12 inches; light/dark distinction only, no real color perception yet.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Offer your finger during calm moments; it's a bonding ritual as much as a reflex check.
entries.doctorFlag: One hand grasps noticeably weaker than the other.
entries.notificationText: Day 13: that death-grip on your finger? 100% reflex, 100% adorable.

entries.description: Many pediatricians see babies around now to confirm weight regain and overall adjustment. Growth, feeding, and jaundice are typically reassessed.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Bring your questions in writing — two weeks in, everyone's running on low sleep and forgets things.
entries.doctorFlag: Persistent yellowing of skin or eyes (jaundice) that seems to be worsening.
entries.notificationText: Day 14: two weeks down. Checkup time to confirm baby's on track.

entries.description: Baby is starting to show clearer preferences for specific soothing techniques — swaddling, rocking, white noise, or a particular hold.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Build a 2-3 move soothing routine now; consistency helps more than novelty at this age.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby cannot be consoled by any method for hours at a stretch, repeatedly.
entries.notificationText: Day 15: baby's telling you which soothing moves actually work.

entries.description: Head and neck control remain very weak — full support is still required for the head at all times during holding and carrying.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Cradle the head deliberately every time you pick baby up; this habit matters for weeks yet.
entries.doctorFlag: Head consistently flops with no resistance even briefly during supported tummy time.
entries.notificationText: Day 16: still zero head control — keep that hand cradling the neck.

entries.description: Newborns can distinguish their mother's scent and voice from others remarkably early, which is part of what makes closeness so calming.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Skin-to-skin time isn't just cozy — it's actively reinforcing recognition and bonding.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby shows no calming response to a familiar caregiver's voice or presence.
entries.notificationText: Day 17: baby already knows your smell in a crowd of strangers.

entries.description: Brief wakeful, alert periods are starting to stretch slightly longer, giving small windows for quiet interaction beyond feeding and diapering.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Use these short awake windows for face time rather than stimulation overload.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby seems either constantly asleep or constantly inconsolable, with no calm-alert state.
entries.notificationText: Day 18: awake windows are (slowly) getting longer. Use them.

entries.description: Baby may follow a slow-moving object or face briefly from side to side, an early and inconsistent form of visual tracking.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Move a face or toy slowly, about a foot away, to give tracking a chance to happen.
entries.doctorFlag: Eyes never move to follow anything, even briefly, across multiple tries.
entries.notificationText: Day 19: eyes are starting to follow — slowly, and not every time.

entries.description: The digestive system is still maturing; spit-up, gas, and irregular stool patterns are common and generally not cause for concern on their own.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Burp mid-feed as well as after; it can meaningfully reduce spit-up and fussiness.
entries.doctorFlag: Projectile vomiting, blood in stool, or a swollen/hard belly.
entries.notificationText: Day 20: digestion is a work in progress — spit-up and all.

entries.description: Around three weeks is a common window for a growth spurt — expect possible cluster feeding (frequent, closely spaced feeds) for a day or two.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Ride out cluster feeding days without assuming your supply has dropped — demand is temporarily up.
entries.doctorFlag: Cluster feeding lasting more than 3-4 days with no letup or weight gain.
entries.notificationText: Day 21: hello growth spurt — expect a hungrier few days.

entries.description: Baby is increasingly likely to calm, at least briefly, when spoken to softly or picked up — an early precursor to the social responsiveness that builds over coming weeks.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Narrate what you're doing in a calm voice while soothing; baby is listening more than it seems.
entries.doctorFlag: No change whatsoever in baby's distress level when picked up and comforted.
entries.notificationText: Day 22: your voice and touch are becoming baby's off-switch.

entries.theme: Sleep cycles shorten and lengthen in turn
entries.description: Newborn sleep cycles run about 50-60 minutes with a high proportion of active (light) sleep, which is why naps often end abruptly.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: A dream feed or gentle re-settle at cycle transitions can sometimes extend a nap.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby cannot be woken at all for feeds despite gentle stimulation, for more than one stretch.
entries.notificationText: Day 23: short sleep cycles aren't a mistake — that's the newborn design.

entries.description: Newborn jaundice, common in the first week, is typically fading by now, and skin tone is evening out as the liver matures.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Natural light near a window (not direct sun) can help you monitor skin tone changes day to day.
entries.doctorFlag: Jaundice that is increasing rather than fading past 2-3 weeks.
entries.notificationText: Day 24: that newborn yellow tinge should be fading about now.

entries.description: The Moro (startle) reflex is still present but may feel marginally less dramatic as baby's nervous system continues maturing.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Swaddling continues to help many babies through the startle reflex phase.
entries.doctorFlag: Startle reflex is absent on one side of the body only.
entries.notificationText: Day 25: startles are still here, just a touch less explosive.

entries.description: Baby is beginning to associate cues — being picked up a certain way, a bib going on — with what comes next, an early form of pattern learning.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Keep pre-feed cues consistent (same chair, same song); repetition is how babies learn sequence.
entries.doctorFlag: No noticeable behavior change in anticipation of feeding cues by one month.
entries.notificationText: Day 26: baby's brain is quietly learning your routines.

entries.description: Brief supervised tummy time is building neck and shoulder strength, even if baby can only lift their head momentarily right now.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: 1-2 minutes, a few times a day, is plenty at this age — short and frequent beats long and rare.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby cries intensely at every tummy time attempt with zero tolerance building over weeks.
entries.notificationText: Day 27: tiny tummy time reps, laying groundwork for head control.

entries.description: Focal distance is slowly extending beyond the original 8-12 inches, letting baby take in slightly more of a room or a nearby toy.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Introduce a mobile or simple toy 12-15 inches away to match this stretching range.
entries.doctorFlag: No visual response to any object or face, even up close, by one month.
entries.notificationText: Day 28: baby's visual world is expanding past your face at last.

entries.description: Early vowel-like coos and grunts may start appearing — the earliest building blocks of vocal communication, well before real babbling.
entries.visionMilestone: Begins brief tracking of faces and bright objects; starting to notice strong color contrasts (especially red).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Learning almost entirely through reflex and sensation; no object permanence yet - once something is out of sight, it's 'gone.'
entries.parentTip: Respond to coos with your own sounds; this back-and-forth is baby's first "conversation."
entries.doctorFlag: Complete absence of any vocal sounds beyond crying by one month.
entries.notificationText: Day 29: baby's making sounds on purpose — the first tiny conversations.

entries.description: By one month, most babies show strong reflexes, brief visual tracking, early coos, and growing alertness. The CDC's 2-month checklist (calming when spoken to, brief face-gazing) is the next big marker to watch for over the coming weeks.
entries.visionMilestone: Follows a moving object or face slowly across the middle of view; broader color vision beginning to develop.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Keep a simple note of feeding, sleep, and any new sound or movement — it's genuinely useful at checkups.
entries.doctorFlag: No progress at all in alertness, feeding, or reflexes compared to the first week.
entries.notificationText: One month down! Reflexes, coos, and eye contact are all coming online.

entries.description: Awake, calm, engaged periods are stretching out, giving more real windows for play and face-to-face interaction between feeds and naps.
entries.visionMilestone: Follows a moving object or face slowly across the middle of view; broader color vision beginning to develop.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Use alert windows for simple back-and-forth: talk, pause, wait for a reaction, repeat.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby seems to have no calm-alert state at all, only feeding or crying.
entries.notificationText: Week 5: awake time is turning into actual playtime.

entries.theme: The first real smile is close
entries.description: Somewhere between 6 and 8 weeks, most babies produce their first true social smile — a response to your face or voice, not a reflex.
entries.visionMilestone: Follows a moving object or face slowly across the middle of view; broader color vision beginning to develop.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Smile and talk face-to-face often this week; you may catch the first intentional smile any day now.
entries.doctorFlag: No hint of a responsive smile by 8 weeks.
entries.notificationText: Week 6: the real social smile could show up any day now.

entries.description: Vowel sounds ('ooh,' 'aah') are becoming more frequent and varied, often produced during calm, alert interaction.
entries.visionMilestone: Follows a moving object or face slowly across the middle of view; broader color vision beginning to develop.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Leave pauses after you talk — babies often 'reply' with a coo if you give them the space.
entries.doctorFlag: No vocal sounds of any kind, including coos, by 7-8 weeks.
entries.notificationText: Week 7: baby's cooing vocabulary is expanding by the day.

entries.description: Tummy time tolerance and head lifts are improving gradually, laying groundwork for the head control gains coming over the next month.
entries.visionMilestone: Follows a moving object or face slowly across the middle of view; broader color vision beginning to develop.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Try tummy time on your chest, reclined, as a gentler alternative if floor time is still tough.
entries.doctorFlag: Zero head lift during tummy time attempts by 2 months.
entries.notificationText: Week 8: neck muscles are quietly leveling up.

entries.description: Around 2 months, most babies calm down when spoken to or picked up, briefly look at your face, smile when you smile at them, and bring their hands to their mouth.
entries.visionMilestone: Smoother, more sustained tracking of movement; recognizes and visually prefers familiar faces at a short distance.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Mention any missing items on this list at the 2-month checkup — it's exactly what pediatricians screen for.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby doesn't respond to loud sounds, doesn't smile at people, or doesn't watch things as they move.
entries.notificationText: 2 months! Face-gazing, social smiles, and hands-to-mouth are the big markers now.

entries.description: Eyes are increasingly following moving objects and people across a room, showing improving visual tracking and attention.
entries.visionMilestone: Smoother, more sustained tracking of movement; recognizes and visually prefers familiar faces at a short distance.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Move slowly across baby's field of view during play — it's genuinely engaging at this stage.
entries.doctorFlag: Eyes consistently fail to track any moving object by 10 weeks.
entries.notificationText: Week 10: baby's eyes are following the action like a tiny spectator.

entries.description: When held upright with feet touching a surface, many babies now push down and briefly bear some weight through their legs.
entries.visionMilestone: Smoother, more sustained tracking of movement; recognizes and visually prefers familiar faces at a short distance.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Try supported standing on your lap for a few seconds — it's fun and builds leg strength.
entries.doctorFlag: Legs remain completely limp with zero weight-bearing attempts by 3 months.
entries.notificationText: Week 11: those legs are starting to push back against the floor.

entries.description: Cooing is turning into more varied vocal play — pitch changes, longer vowel strings, and clear enjoyment of making sounds.
entries.visionMilestone: Smoother, more sustained tracking of movement; recognizes and visually prefers familiar faces at a short distance.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Starting to notice simple cause-and-effect (a kick moves the mobile); repeats actions that produced a pleasing result.
entries.parentTip: Mimic baby's sounds back to them; imitation is one of the earliest language 'games.'
entries.doctorFlag: No variation at all in vocal sounds, only crying, by 3 months.
entries.notificationText: Week 12: baby's discovered their voice has range.

entries.description: By around 3 months, most babies show a fuller social smile, track objects smoothly, hold their head fairly steady, and push up onto their forearms during tummy time.
entries.visionMilestone: Fuller color vision emerging; both eyes coordinating together, giving early depth perception (stereopsis).
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Actions become more intentional; anticipates familiar routines, like turning toward a bottle before it's offered.
entries.parentTip: This is a natural pause point to compare baby's overall trajectory against the 2-month checklist milestones.
entries.doctorFlag: Persistent head lag when pulled to sit, or no social smile at all by 3 months.
entries.notificationText: 3 months in — head control, tracking, and smiles are all leveling up.

entries.description: The CDC's 4-month markers include smiling on their own to get your attention, chuckling when you play with them, following objects with their eyes, bringing hands to mouth, and pushing up onto their arms during tummy time.
entries.visionMilestone: Reaches accurately for nearby objects using improving hand-eye and depth coordination.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Actions become more intentional; anticipates familiar routines, like turning toward a bottle before it's offered.
entries.parentTip: Play simple back-and-forth games (peekaboo, silly faces) — they directly target several of these markers at once.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby doesn't smile at people, doesn't bring hands to mouth, or can't hold their head steady by 4 months.
entries.notificationText: 4 months: chuckles, self-initiated smiles, and stronger arm pushes are the theme.

entries.description: Rolling from tummy to back (and sometimes back to tummy) often emerges around now, along with more deliberate reaching for toys.
entries.visionMilestone: Reaches accurately for nearby objects using improving hand-eye and depth coordination.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Actions become more intentional; anticipates familiar routines, like turning toward a bottle before it's offered.
entries.parentTip: Give plenty of supervised floor time — rolling practice needs open, safe space.
entries.doctorFlag: No rolling in either direction and no reaching for objects by 5-6 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 5: rolling practice is officially underway.

entries.description: By 6 months, most babies roll in both directions, move objects from hand to hand, know familiar faces, enjoy mirrors, and blow 'raspberries.' Sitting with support is common, unsupported sitting is emerging.
entries.visionMilestone: Judges distances well for a baby; visually inspects objects up close and shifts gaze quickly between them.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Explores objects by mouthing, banging, and shaking; beginning to grasp very simple cause-and-effect toys.
entries.parentTip: A stable, supported sitting position opens up a whole new play perspective — worth prioritizing floor time in this position.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby doesn't roll in either direction or shows no interest in reaching for things by 6 months.
entries.notificationText: 6 months: rolling both ways, hand-to-hand transfers, mirror giggles.

entries.description: Unsupported sitting is becoming steadier for many babies, freeing up hands for more sustained toy exploration.
entries.visionMilestone: Judges distances well for a baby; visually inspects objects up close and shifts gaze quickly between them.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Explores objects by mouthing, banging, and shaking; beginning to grasp very simple cause-and-effect toys.
entries.parentTip: Surround baby with soft support (cushions/your legs) while they practice sitting solo.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby cannot sit even briefly with support by 7-8 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 7: sitting solo is opening up a whole new toy-grabbing era.

entries.description: Many babies start exploring mobility now — some crawl, others scoot, roll, or go straight to pulling up; the CDC no longer treats crawling itself as a required milestone since paths to mobility vary widely.
entries.visionMilestone: Judges distances well for a baby; visually inspects objects up close and shifts gaze quickly between them.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Explores objects by mouthing, banging, and shaking; beginning to grasp very simple cause-and-effect toys.
entries.parentTip: Baby-proof low shelves and cords now, even if baby isn't fully mobile yet — it happens fast once it starts.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in moving toward objects or people by any method (crawling, scooting, rolling) by 9 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 8: mobility is starting, however baby chooses to get there.

entries.description: CDC 9-month markers include sitting without support, moving objects hand to hand, looking when their name is called, playing peekaboo, and picking up small items between finger and thumb.
entries.visionMilestone: Depth perception largely established; vision guides the fine pincer grasp used to pick up small objects.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Object permanence is emerging - actively looks for a dropped or partly hidden toy; understands a few words in context.
entries.parentTip: Practice name-calling from across the room during play — it's a fun, low-effort way to reinforce this milestone.
entries.doctorFlag: Baby doesn't sit with support, doesn't babble, or doesn't respond to their own name by 9 months.
entries.notificationText: 9 months: name recognition, peekaboo, and pincer-grasp beginnings.

entries.description: Pulling to stand and 'cruising' — walking while holding onto furniture — is common now, alongside an increasingly precise pincer grasp for small objects.
entries.visionMilestone: Depth perception largely established; vision guides the fine pincer grasp used to pick up small objects.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Object permanence is emerging - actively looks for a dropped or partly hidden toy; understands a few words in context.
entries.parentTip: Line up sturdy, low furniture to create a safe cruising path around the room.
entries.doctorFlag: No pulling to stand attempts and no interest in standing with support by 10-11 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 10: furniture cruising has officially begun.

entries.description: Nonspecific 'mama' and 'dada' sounds, and simple gestures like waving, are increasingly common as babies bridge from babble toward real words.
entries.visionMilestone: Depth perception largely established; vision guides the fine pincer grasp used to pick up small objects.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Object permanence is emerging - actively looks for a dropped or partly hidden toy; understands a few words in context.
entries.parentTip: Label objects constantly during daily routines — repetition is how word meaning eventually clicks.
entries.doctorFlag: No babbling of any consonant-vowel combinations by 11-12 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 11: mama, dada, and waves are entering the repertoire.

entries.description: CDC 12-month markers: pulling to stand, walking while holding onto furniture, saying 'mama'/'dada' with meaning, waving bye-bye, and trying to say one or two words beyond mama/dada.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Celebrate this one — the first year is the single fastest stretch of change in the entire 2,000 days.
entries.doctorFlag: No words, no gestures like waving, and no attempts to stand by 12-14 months.
entries.notificationText: Happy 1st birthday! Words, waves, and wobbly standing are all arriving.

entries.description: Cruising is often becoming more confident, and some babies take their first independent, unsupported steps around now — though the range here is wide.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Let baby practice barefoot on safe, flat surfaces — it helps with balance feedback.
entries.doctorFlag: No cruising or weight-bearing on legs at all by 14-15 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 13: independent steps might happen any day now.

entries.description: Vocabulary is typically building slowly — a handful of clear or near-clear words used consistently for the same thing.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Narrate your day out loud; incidental exposure to language matters as much as direct teaching.
entries.doctorFlag: Zero consistent words (not even 'mama/dada' with meaning) by 15 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 14: a small but growing word bank is forming.

entries.description: CDC 15-month markers: walking without holding on to anyone, using several gestures, trying to say three or more words, following one-step directions, and drinking from an open cup.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Offer a small open cup at meals occasionally — it's messy practice, but it's exactly what's being tracked here.
entries.doctorFlag: Not walking at all, or fewer than 3 attempted words, by 15-18 months.
entries.notificationText: 15 months: walking, first real words, and cup-drinking practice.

entries.description: Fine motor control is advancing — scribbling with a crayon and stacking two blocks are common new tricks.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Keep crayons and blocks accessible; open-ended play is doing more developmental work than it looks.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in or ability to grasp and manipulate small objects by 16-18 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 16: scribbles and block towers (of two) are the new hobby.

entries.description: Imitation is ramping up — mimicking simple household actions like wiping, stirring, or 'talking' on a toy phone.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Narrate chores as you do them; imitation play is a major learning channel at this age.
entries.doctorFlag: No imitation of any adult actions or gestures by 18 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 17: baby's turned into your tiny copycat.

entries.description: CDC 18-month markers: walking up steps while holding on, pointing to show you something interesting, following one-step directions without gestures, and moving away from then returning to a caregiver.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Practice simple one-step requests ('bring me the ball') without pointing or gesturing to see this milestone in action.
entries.doctorFlag: No pointing to show interest, and vocabulary stuck at zero words, by 18-20 months.
entries.notificationText: 18 months: pointing, stair-climbing, and independent exploring.

entries.description: Early two-word combinations ('more milk,' 'go car') may start appearing as vocabulary crosses roughly 20-50 words for many toddlers.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Expand rather than correct: if they say 'more milk,' you can reply 'yes, more milk please.'
entries.doctorFlag: No word combinations attempted and vocabulary under 10 words by 20-24 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 19: two-word phrases are starting to click into place.

entries.description: Gross motor skills are advancing quickly — running (if a bit stiff-legged), kicking a ball, and climbing onto low furniture.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Open, safe outdoor space is genuinely valuable now; big-body movement is a major developmental driver.
entries.doctorFlag: No running or attempted kicking motion by 22-24 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 20: stiff-legged running and ball-kicking have arrived.

entries.description: Parallel play — playing near, but not really with, other children — is typical now, an important social stepping stone.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Playdates are valuable even if the kids barely interact directly; proximity play still teaches social skills.
entries.doctorFlag: Active distress or complete disinterest around all other children by 24 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 21: parallel play — together, but doing their own thing.

entries.description: Basic sorting by shape or color is emerging, along with simple puzzles and matching games.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Simple shape sorters and chunky puzzles are well-matched to this exact stage.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in matching, sorting, or simple cause-and-effect toys by 24 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 22: sorting shapes and colors is the new favorite game.

entries.description: Growing independence often brings more frequent tantrums — a normal (if exhausting) part of toddlers testing autonomy against limits.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity steadily maturing (roughly 20/40-20/60 range); enjoys and points at pictures in books.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Trial-and-error problem-solving appears; imitates actions seen earlier; first stirrings of pretend play.
entries.parentTip: Stay calm and consistent with limits; tantrums are communication, not manipulation, at this age.
entries.doctorFlag: Tantrums involving self-injury or lasting well over 25 minutes routinely.
entries.notificationText: Month 23: big opinions, bigger feelings — the toddler era in full swing.

entries.description: CDC 24-month markers: kicking a ball, running, walking up/down stairs while holding on, eating with a spoon, saying two-word phrases, sorting shapes and colors, following two-step instructions, and noticing when others are hurt or upset.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: The empathy marker (noticing others' distress) is worth watching for directly — point out feelings in books and daily life to reinforce it.
entries.doctorFlag: Fewer than 50 words, no two-word phrases, or no interest in others by 24-30 months.
entries.notificationText: 2 years old! Running, spoons, two-word phrases, and early empathy.

entries.description: Pretend play is expanding — feeding a doll, pretending to talk on the phone, or acting out simple scenes from daily life.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Join pretend play as a participant, not just an observer; it builds language and imagination together.
entries.doctorFlag: No pretend or symbolic play attempts at all by 30 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 25: make-believe is officially open for business.

entries.description: Many toddlers cross into rapid vocabulary growth around now, sometimes adding new words daily and combining them into longer phrases.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Keep reading aloud daily — vocabulary growth at this stage is strongly tied to language exposure.
entries.doctorFlag: Vocabulary essentially frozen at under 50 words by 30 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 26: word count is climbing fast now.

entries.description: Jumping with both feet off the ground together is a new gross motor trick many toddlers attempt around now, along with improved balance overall.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Cushions or a low step are great low-stakes jump practice spots.
entries.doctorFlag: No attempt at jumping and noticeably unsteady walking by 30 months.
entries.notificationText: Month 27: two feet, one jump — a new physical milestone.

entries.description: Curiosity ramps up with frequent 'why' questions — a sign of expanding reasoning and cause-and-effect thinking, even if it's exhausting to answer.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Simple, honest answers work better than long explanations at this age — you can always expand later.
entries.doctorFlag: No curiosity-driven questions of any kind by 3 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 28: 'why' has entered the vocabulary — buckle up.

entries.description: Naming at least one or two colors correctly and consistently is a common new skill in this window.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Point out colors casually during everyday moments rather than formal 'lessons.'
entries.doctorFlag: No color recognition attempts and no interest in naming objects by 3 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 29: colors are getting names now.

entries.description: CDC 30-month markers: using two-to-three word sentences, following two-step instructions, naming items in a picture book, playing next to and sometimes with other children, and noticing when someone is hurt or upset.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Picture books with simple, repetitive stories are ideal practice ground for several of these markers at once.
entries.doctorFlag: No two-word sentences and no interest in playing near other children by 30-36 months.
entries.notificationText: 30 months: two-to-three word sentences and picture-book naming.

entries.description: Turn-taking is emerging, though usually still needing adult prompting and reminders — a foundation for later cooperative play.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Simple turn-taking games (rolling a ball back and forth) build this skill more naturally than lectures about sharing.
entries.doctorFlag: Extreme, unrelenting distress at any turn-taking attempt by 3 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 31: turn-taking practice — with plenty of reminders needed.

entries.description: Fine motor and visual-spatial skills are advancing enough for some toddlers to attempt drawing a circle or simple closed shape.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Open-ended drawing time (no 'right way') builds confidence alongside the skill itself.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in or ability to hold a crayon by 3 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 32: circles are starting to show up on paper.

entries.description: Basic counting — reciting numbers, sometimes with accurate one-to-one correspondence for a couple of items — is emerging for many toddlers.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Count everyday objects together (stairs, snacks) rather than drilling numbers abstractly.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in numbers or counting attempts by 3.5 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 33: 1, 2, 3 — counting is entering the picture.

entries.description: Coordination for pedaling a tricycle is developing, along with generally smoother, more confident running and climbing.
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: A tricycle or balance bike is well-matched to this stage's emerging leg coordination.
entries.doctorFlag: Significant clumsiness or falling with every attempt at running by 3.5 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 34: tricycle pedaling is the next physical frontier.

entries.description: Grammar basics — plurals, past tense, simple pronouns — are starting to show up in speech, even with plenty of charming errors ('I goed').
entries.visionMilestone: Can identify and name simple pictures; eyes track a fast-moving object like a rolled or thrown ball.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Symbolic thinking blooms - full pretend play, grasps 'one' versus 'many,' short-term memory improving noticeably.
entries.parentTip: Model correct grammar gently by repeating back correctly, without directly correcting the child.
entries.doctorFlag: Speech that's largely unintelligible even to familiar caregivers by 3 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 35: grammar is sneaking into sentences, errors and all.

entries.description: CDC 3-year markers: climbing well, running easily, pedaling a tricycle, speaking in 3-4 word sentences that strangers can mostly understand, engaging in make-believe, playing with other children, dressing/undressing partly independently, and naming a friend.
entries.visionMilestone: Acuity approaching near-adult levels; can copy simple shapes by sight and match colors accurately.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Reasoning is curious but egocentric (constant 'why'); starts classifying objects by a single feature like color or size.
entries.parentTip: Naming a friend is a nice one to ask about directly — it's a good window into social development at this stage.
entries.doctorFlag: Speech mostly unintelligible to strangers, or no interest in other children, by 3.5-4 years.
entries.notificationText: 3 years old! Sentences, make-believe, tricycles, and a first named friend.

entries.description: Sentences are lengthening and grammar is getting steadily more accurate, with growing use of connecting words like 'and' and 'but.'
entries.visionMilestone: Acuity approaching near-adult levels; can copy simple shapes by sight and match colors accurately.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Reasoning is curious but egocentric (constant 'why'); starts classifying objects by a single feature like color or size.
entries.parentTip: Ask open-ended questions ('what happened at the park?') to encourage longer verbal responses.
entries.doctorFlag: Sentences remain 2 words or fewer, or speech is largely unintelligible, by 4 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 39: sentences are stretching out, connectors and all.

entries.description: Many children can briefly hop on one foot now, and simple storytelling — a beginning, middle, and end — is emerging in play and conversation.
entries.visionMilestone: Acuity approaching near-adult levels; can copy simple shapes by sight and match colors accurately.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Reasoning is curious but egocentric (constant 'why'); starts classifying objects by a single feature like color or size.
entries.parentTip: Ask your child to 'tell you what happened' after an outing; it builds narrative skills directly.
entries.doctorFlag: No attempt at hopping or one-leg balance at all by 4 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 42: one-foot hops and the first real stories.

entries.description: Catching a large, softly-thrown ball is becoming more reliable, and drawings often now include a person with two or three body parts.
entries.visionMilestone: Acuity approaching near-adult levels; can copy simple shapes by sight and match colors accurately.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Reasoning is curious but egocentric (constant 'why'); starts classifying objects by a single feature like color or size.
entries.parentTip: A soft, large ball is more forgiving for catching practice than anything smaller.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in ball games and no attempt at drawing a person by 4 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 45: ball-catching and 'person drawings' are developing.

entries.description: CDC 4-year markers: hopping on one foot, catching a ball most of the time, pouring and cutting with supervision, using 'and' to join sentences, answering simple 'why' questions, and trying to comfort others who are upset.
entries.visionMilestone: Good visual-motor integration - catches a ball reliably, begins cutting along a line, recognizes some letters by sight.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Loose grasp of time concepts (today/tomorrow); counts with real meaning for small numbers; follows a story's logical order.
entries.parentTip: The 'comforts others' marker is worth actively noticing — point out others' feelings often to nurture this budding empathy.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in pretend play, no 'why' question answers, or ignores other children entirely by 5 years.
entries.notificationText: 4 years old! Hopping, ball skills, and comforting others when they're sad.

entries.description: Drawing basic shapes (squares, crosses) and counting up to around 10 with reasonable accuracy are common developments now.
entries.visionMilestone: Good visual-motor integration - catches a ball reliably, begins cutting along a line, recognizes some letters by sight.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Loose grasp of time concepts (today/tomorrow); counts with real meaning for small numbers; follows a story's logical order.
entries.parentTip: Simple board games with counting and shapes turn practice into play.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in shapes, numbers, or letters at all by 5 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 51: shapes and counting to 10 are taking shape.

entries.description: Self-care independence — dressing, basic hygiene, simple food prep help — and emotional vocabulary ('I feel frustrated') are both expanding.
entries.visionMilestone: Good visual-motor integration - catches a ball reliably, begins cutting along a line, recognizes some letters by sight.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Loose grasp of time concepts (today/tomorrow); counts with real meaning for small numbers; follows a story's logical order.
entries.parentTip: Offer real choices ('red shirt or blue shirt?') to nurture independence within safe limits.
entries.doctorFlag: Complete inability to attempt any self-care task, or no emotional vocabulary at all, by 5 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 54: dressing solo and naming feelings are both leveling up.

entries.description: Speech is typically fluent and complex now, with children readily telling multi-step imaginative stories and asking increasingly detailed questions.
entries.visionMilestone: Good visual-motor integration - catches a ball reliably, begins cutting along a line, recognizes some letters by sight.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Loose grasp of time concepts (today/tomorrow); counts with real meaning for small numbers; follows a story's logical order.
entries.parentTip: Ask follow-up questions in their stories ('then what happened?') — it's genuinely engaging for both of you.
entries.doctorFlag: Speech that remains hard for strangers to understand by 5.5 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 57: full storyteller mode has arrived.

entries.description: CDC 5-year markers: following rules and taking turns in games, doing simple chores, hopping and skipping, using a fork and sometimes a table knife, counting to 10 or beyond, naming some letters and numbers, drawing a person with at least 3 body parts, and telling a story with full sentences.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity typically reaches 20/20; ready for sustained close-up visual tasks like early reading and writing.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Can hold multi-step instructions in mind; early theory of mind (starting to grasp that others feel/think differently); pre-literacy and pre-numeracy skills solidifying.
entries.parentTip: This is a natural point for a kindergarten-readiness conversation with your pediatrician or child's teacher.
entries.doctorFlag: Cannot follow simple game rules, cannot count to 10, or speech remains largely unintelligible by 6 years.
entries.notificationText: 5 years old! Chores, skipping, storytelling, and near-kindergarten skills.

entries.description: Letter recognition, rhyming awareness, and interest in 'reading' familiar books independently (often from memory) are building pre-literacy foundations.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity typically reaches 20/20; ready for sustained close-up visual tasks like early reading and writing.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Can hold multi-step instructions in mind; early theory of mind (starting to grasp that others feel/think differently); pre-literacy and pre-numeracy skills solidifying.
entries.parentTip: Point to words as you read aloud; it quietly builds the connection between spoken and written language.
entries.doctorFlag: No interest in books, letters, or rhyming by 5.5-6 years.
entries.notificationText: Month 63: pre-reading skills are quietly clicking into place.

entries.description: Somewhere around now marks day 2,000 since birth — roughly five and a half years of the fastest, most concentrated developmental growth a human ever experiences. Independent friendships, complex pretend play, expanding self-regulation, and near-kindergarten academic readiness are the hallmarks of this stretch.
entries.visionMilestone: Visual acuity typically reaches 20/20; ready for sustained close-up visual tasks like early reading and writing.
entries.cognitiveMilestone: Can hold multi-step instructions in mind; early theory of mind (starting to grasp that others feel/think differently); pre-literacy and pre-numeracy skills solidifying.
entries.parentTip: Look back at day 1 versus today — it's worth pausing on how much ground has actually been covered.
entries.doctorFlag: Any persistent concern about speech, social connection, or motor skills deserves a conversation with your pediatrician, regardless of day count.
entries.notificationText: Day 2,000 reached — from newborn reflexes to a full-fledged little person. What a journey.
